turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: We'll hear argument now in Number oh oh seven fifty-eight, United States Postal Service versus Maria A Gregory. Mr. Garre. [Inaudible]

turns.name: GREGORY G. GARRE
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court. For decades Federal employers and the Merit Systems Protection Board have engaged in the common sense practice of considering an employee's prior disciplinary record in deciding what punishment is appropriate for subsequent misconduct. The settled practice has long been to do so, even when a prior disciplinary action is subject to a pending labor grievance, although in that context, the board permits the employee to collaterally attack the prior actions in proceedings before it. In this case, the Federal circuit...

turns.name: GREGORY G. GARRE
turns.text: overtur- If there...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: is a collateral attack, the employee says, look, I filed a grievance and it's pending, what then is the burden of proof by the Government employer?

turns.name: GREGORY G. GARRE
turns.text: The Government employer bears the burden of proving the action by a preponderance of the evidence.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: But what what is it with regard to the prior offenses, if you will, that have for which grievance procedures have been filed? How is that then addressed?

turns.name: GREGORY G. GARRE
turns.text: Under the loun- longstanding framework, which is established by the board's Bolling decision, the employer has to prove the fact of the prior action, and he has to, and the employer has to im- prove that it was preceded by certain procedural protections, first, that the employee received advance notice of the action, second, that the employee had an opportunity to respond to the charges before the supervisor, as well as by a higher authority within the agency, and third, that there was a record...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: proceeding...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: mean somebody higher up the ladder in the employing agency?

turns.name: GREGORY G. GARRE
turns.text: It does, a- and that's and th- that's the same type of challenge that is framed in the early stages of the grievance process.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: Well, what if the final action, the decision to terminate the services of the employee what if it had been based on the commission of some prior failure as an employee and the grievance procedure had proceeded and it had been determined that it was invalid?

turns.name: GREGORY G. GARRE
turns.text: In that circumstance, the board has held that it would be inappropriate to rely on that action to defend the subsequent action.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: disciplinary actions...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: What happens...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: fact, here three preceding incidents, as I recall, and a grievance was filed on all of them.

turns.name: GREGORY G. GARRE
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: disciplinary action.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: Justice O'Connor's example? And let me add one thing, that the prior disciplinary actions are being grieved.

turns.name: GREGORY G. GARRE
turns.text: Well, first of all, there's grievances can be pressed before, during, and after appeals before the Merit Systems Protection Board.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: the appeal...

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: the answer to my question?

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: It...

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: question was do you, should I repeat it or you have it?

turns.name: GREGORY G. GARRE
turns.text: i-, My understanding is that in, even when a grievance is proceeding, the board would would proceed with the processing of its...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: appeal.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: question is take the present situation.

turns.name: GREGORY G. GARRE
turns.text: I'm sorry.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: basis for infal-

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: to my hypothetical, it can't happen.

turns.name: GREGORY G. GARRE
turns.text: The board can't go to an arbitrator and tell him...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: to stop.

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: what about the employer? Can the postal department s- d- say , well, you know, this this sh- sh- she's not an employee here anyway, we're, you don't need to continue this? Or is it y- you you leave me with the impression, rightly or wrongly, that this this i- the that this is just at the option of the union.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: It's...

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: proceed if it wants or doesn't have to proceed.

turns.name: GREGORY G. GARRE
turns.text: That's a matter covered by the collective bargaining agree- arrangement.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: that employers can't...

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: but what we're trying to establish and I think you would have to concede that there are some instances in which once the employee is terminated, the grievance proceedings as to other matters must stop.

turns.name: GREGORY G. GARRE
turns.text: That can happen and it happens at,

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: because of...

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: just because it's the option of the union other than what the union agreed to in the collective bargaining agreement.

turns.name: GREGORY G. GARRE
turns.text: The arrangement is the the grievances are withdrawn by the union or they're ri- withdrawn under the arrangement that's been worked out under the...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: collective bargaining agreement.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: collective...

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: bargaining agreement is silent.

turns.name: GREGORY G. GARRE
turns.text: That that would be a decision left to the arbitrator under the framework of the negotiated grievance.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: can happen. And...

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: e- if it's c- covered by the collective bargaining agreement, there's no choice in the matter, the grievance is stopped.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Now, this may not be...

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: fatal to your case, but if this happens, I think we should confront it.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: And Justice Breyer...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: this would happen.

turns.name: GREGORY G. GARRE
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: And the union could say, you know, I think you deserved it and and we're not going to proceed any further.

turns.name: GREGORY G. GARRE
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: leaves to the...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: is not an inju- if if that if that's what it is, is not an injustice peculiar to this arrangements.

turns.name: GREGORY G. GARRE
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Mr. Garre?

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: In civil litigation generally, when there when there's a proceeding that's dependent on a prior proceeding that's on appeal, the standard operating procedure is for the second proceeding to be held at abeyance pending the appeal of the first.

turns.name: GREGORY G. GARRE
turns.text: It's it's very problematic.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: estoppel effect. But...

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: doesn't prevent it, but it is the standard proceeding to hold the second one in abeyance.

turns.name: GREGORY G. GARRE
turns.text: But...

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: So that you don't have the the anomaly of giving effect to a judgment that has been overturned.

turns.name: GREGORY G. GARRE
turns.text: Th- There are several problems with the abeyance rule adopted by the Federal circuit, which is essentially the narrow rule which is hypothesized by respondent.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Why if i-

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: why, if the agency action goes into effect immediately? What what I'm presenting to you is the the thing goes through the agency.

turns.name: GREGORY G. GARRE
turns.text: Well, first of all, although the employee is paced in, placed in a non-duty, non-pay status when she's removed by the agency, she continues to fill a permanent slot on the agency's rolls, and if the abeyance rule is going to require agencies to keep the employee in that position for months, if not years, on end, that's problematic from the employer's perspective.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: the abeyance...

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: i- i- would you explain that if to me? Two, why would the why if the firing is is okay, at least until it's overturned, why couldn't the the agency fill the vacancy?

turns.name: GREGORY G. GARRE
turns.text: The the employee is placed under the practice followed by the Postal Service and I believe other employers as well, the employee occupies the full-time slot, and until her removal is affirmed by the board, she continues to fill that slot.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: on end. It also...

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: May may I just ask you to get into this problem of months and years? The months and years problem I I understand is is simply a function of of what you claim to be the slow pace of arbitration.

turns.name: GREGORY G. GARRE
turns.text: I I think they have tried, but the fact is that in the Postal Service, there's currently a hundred and twenty-six thousand grievances pending in that process, backlogged.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: And this is the situation that...

turns.name: GREGORY G. GARRE
turns.text: Well, this is the situation that exists, and the board isn't required to hold its appeals in abeyance while that...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: procedure is played out.

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: my my poin- I guess my point is that you say this is the situation that exists.

turns.name: GREGORY G. GARRE
turns.text: Well, first of all, the inherent informality of the grievance and arbitration process is always going to invite delay.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Well, litigation...

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: invites delay.

turns.name: GREGORY G. GARRE
turns.text: Well, that hasn't happened, and I don't think it's unique to the Postal Service ar- arbitration context.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Mr. Garre, d-

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: d- do you think that for purposes of either collateral estoppel or for purposes of whether a criminal court can use a prior conviction in deciding the sentence d- do you think it it's accurate to analogize the grievance procedure as a prior case pending on appeal? Or would you rather characterize it as a collateral attack...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: I think it's...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: final action by the employer?

turns.name: GREGORY G. GARRE
turns.text: I I think it's the latter.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: proceeding to...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: and what happens to collateral attacks when you have a final criminal conviction and there is a collateral attack on that criminal conviction, although the conviction itself is final? When a sentencing court has that conviction before it, does it not use that conviction.

turns.name: GREGORY G. GARRE
turns.text: Absolutely.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: And if the collateral attack is later successful, what is what is the remedy for the...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: person who's been convicted?

turns.name: GREGORY G. GARRE
turns.text: they can bring it to the attention of the...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: court and ask for relief.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: to reopen the proceeding.

turns.name: GREGORY G. GARRE
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: And that and that...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: do here? We have a case here where, as I understand it, there were three prior complaints by the employer that resulted in some form of disciplinary action.

turns.name: GREGORY G. GARRE
turns.text: Well, we think that this Court should decide the question presented, reverse the decision below, and remand for further proceedings,

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: allow the Federal circuit...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: to the other two grievances...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: The other two...

turns.name: GREGORY G. GARRE
turns.text: grievances were withdrawn by the union when the board affirmed the the removal.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: This the Federal circuit...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: remand necessary? What i- i- if we agree with with you on the merits, what what remains to be decided by the Federal circuit?

turns.name: GREGORY G. GARRE
turns.text: The the issue that that remains open is the question of what effect the grievance that has been set aside should have on the board's decision affirming the removal.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: address...

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: You say if we reverse on the question presented, we should leave it to the Federal circuit to decide whether to direct reopening of the proceedings or to affirm the MSPB?

turns.name: GREGORY G. GARRE
turns.text: We we th- we th- that's what we would ask this Court to do.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: There's a disagreement between you and the respondent, is there not, about whether the m- b- the agency, the Postal Service, is bound or whether it's only the MSPB.

turns.name: GREGORY G. GARRE
turns.text: Well, we we think that the fairest reading of the court of appeals decision, as it applies both to Federal employers and the Merit Systems Protection Board, the holding of the court is unqualified.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: proceedings.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: Their their point on that particularly is what were you is, all right the the particular point I think on that is that the the Federal circuit really just said the M the MSPB.

turns.name: GREGORY G. GARRE
turns.text: Well, first of all, we don't we don't think that's the fairest interpretation of the court of appeals decision.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: That's one of the problems...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: d- does the Government acknowledge that the MSPB can determine that it is arbitrary, capricious, or an abuse of discretion for the agency to decline to reopen a proceeding after a grievance has gone forward and has found one of the convictions on which the dismissal is based to have been invalid?

turns.name: GREGORY G. GARRE
turns.text: The the the board's regulations permit the board to reopen any case at any time to reconsider it in light of a grievance which may have proved successful.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: to request...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: regulations permit that?

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: board's right? Doesn't the board have to find that the agency action, in refusing to reopen, is arbitrary, capricious, or an abuse of discretion? I mean, the board can say when it will reopen it's own cases, but but the board can't tell the agency when the agency must reopen its cases, can it, unless the failure to reopen is arbitrary or capricious?

turns.name: GREGORY G. GARRE
turns.text: No.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: board's reopening rule.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: talking about ultimate success in the grievance and then then the employee comes back to the agency and says, look, you you goofed.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Well, certainly in that...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: board find that to be an abuse of discretion?

turns.name: GREGORY G. GARRE
turns.text: That's the the typical practice is that the employee will go to the board and say, reopen my appeal because this grievance has proved successful.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: So, typically...

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: PB rule, that you if if while the MSPB thing is still going on, one of the grievances e- the MSPB, if the person hasn't been fired, will reconsider.

turns.name: GREGORY G. GARRE
turns.text: The the employee may do that, and the board may do that.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: But it's discretionary, isn't it? That the board doesn't have to reopen if it doesn't want to.

turns.name: GREGORY G. GARRE
turns.text: It is discretionary, and that's really no different than any other reopening procedure which would exist to to enable a court or other body to reconsider something in light of subsequent evidence.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: that.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: on the proposition that they are not final in in in the sense that that that a a judicial determination is final.

turns.name: GREGORY G. GARRE
turns.text: We think that they are final.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: I don't want to consume your...

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: rebuttal time, but there's one question that that that that still hasn't been answered for me.

turns.name: GREGORY G. GARRE
turns.text: We think that the board can take those into account under the Bolling framework, which allows the board to consider the prior disciplinary actions under a procedural perfr- framework which looks to the procedural protections provided in those proceedings and then considers whether those actions...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: What would your...

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: position be before the Postal Service as to the union's request to reopen those?

turns.name: GREGORY G. GARRE
turns.text: To reopen the?

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: Those to or to continue those un- una- those pretermitted grievance proceedings.

turns.name: GREGORY G. GARRE
turns.text: Those arbitrations could go forward.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: that per-

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: no objection to those going forward?

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: The arbi- the...

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: I thought the union was obje-

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: wanting...

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: to go forward, the and the Postal Service didn't.

turns.name: GREGORY G. GARRE
turns.text: Under the memorandum that's in place, if the respondent's removal were affirmed by the board, then under the memorandum in place, the union's practice is to withdraw those agreements under the collective bargaining arrangement.

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: Mr. Garre, apropos of the remand, you mentioned that the respondent had not brought to the board's attention the fact that the arbitrator had ordered the first disciplinary action vacated.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: of the board?

turns.name: GREGORY G. GARRE
turns.text: bring that to the attention of the board.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Garre.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: Mr. Brands, we'll hear from you.

turns.name: HENK BRANDS
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: The MSPB --

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: talking now about review before the MSPB, not before the Federal circuit.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Is that correct?

turns.name: HENK BRANDS
turns.text: The MSPB has held that whenever the agency comes before the MSPB and relies on aggravating facts, not necessarily the particular charge before the MSPB, but other things, for example, the employee is simply not very good or or something like that, that has to be proven as well by by a preponderance de novo in the MSPB.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: I do not...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: even if a grievance is not pending, the board would have to review the prior disciplinary action to be sure that that was supported by a preponderance of the evidence? Surely it surely it doesn't mean that.

turns.name: HENK BRANDS
turns.text: Well, Justice Scalia, I think that the burden of proof is always the same.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Yeah, but the burden of proof is this employee has been convicted of prior disciplinary violations in the past.

turns.name: HENK BRANDS
turns.text: And...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: QED, proven.

turns.name: HENK BRANDS
turns.text: Certainly when...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: But you're you're...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: saying that's not enough.

turns.name: HENK BRANDS
turns.text: No, Justice Scalia, we're not saying that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: But that is not...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: same presumption still valid even though the the action is being grieved?

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Well, two things...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: not as though it were unilateral employer action without a hearing and procedural guarantees.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: That is certainly...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: judgment is final until un- un- and what you're grieving is employer action, and and that action is final until a grievance overturns it.

turns.name: HENK BRANDS
turns.text: Justice Scalia, I think that the analogy that the Government would like to draw is to an agency action that is subject to section seven oh six review under the APA, but that is not the right analogy, we respectfully submit.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: Well, what we're reviewing here, Mr. Brands, is not the Merit Systems Protection Board's action, but the action the Federal circuit.

turns.name: HENK BRANDS
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: As I understand it, the Federal circuit here said this was not in accordance with law.

turns.name: HENK BRANDS
turns.text: Well, that is true, and perhaps the brevity of the a- of the discussion by the court of appeals can be explained on the ground of of the fact that the Government did not respond to any of respondent's arguments in the court of appeals.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: proof.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: but you you you say it's u- not in accordance with law, and then in the same sentence, you say it arbitrarily disregards the burden of proof.

turns.name: HENK BRANDS
turns.text: And that's...

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: a- and which I don't think the Federal circuit did.

turns.name: HENK BRANDS
turns.text: That is correct, but nevertheless, its judgment, we think, is is right on the money.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: Now, the the that's that's s- standard you say governs the Merit Systems Protection Board?

turns.name: HENK BRANDS
turns.text: That is correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: so on and so...

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: the basis for the Federal circuit's decision here.

turns.name: HENK BRANDS
turns.text: We think it actually was, Your Honor, because...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: under...

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: Where d- where do you find that?

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: HENK BRANDS
turns.text: the petition appendix seven A where the court of appeals said that its rule is necessary because the foundation of the board of the MSPB's Douglas analysis would otherwise be compromised.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: is...

turns.name: HENK BRANDS
turns.text: And again, we would we would suggest that the del- telegraphic nature of that may may also be blamed on the Government's conduct of...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: the litigation in the court of appeals.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: blamed on the Government?

turns.name: HENK BRANDS
turns.text: Well, let let me just explain real quick what we think that the court of appeals meant by that.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: I thought you already conceded in in response to an earlier question of mine that y- that the that the board does not have to establish by a preponderance of the evidence the accuracy of any prior disciplinary conviction.

turns.name: HENK BRANDS
turns.text: I don't think I said that, Your Honor.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Oh, I thought you did.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: If you -- then why isn't it true? Suppose there had...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: been no grievance.

turns.name: HENK BRANDS
turns.text: Well, the the statutory burden, of course, always applies.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: So, your answer is yes.

turns.name: HENK BRANDS
turns.text: No, it's not necessarily yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Well.

turns.name: HENK BRANDS
turns.text: I would distinguish between three factual scenarios.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: proving...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: to he or she.

turns.name: HENK BRANDS
turns.text: No, that's actually not entirely true.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: the right to do that.

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: still that isn't this case.

turns.name: HENK BRANDS
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: No, because this in this case we have a grievance that was not fully determined.

turns.name: HENK BRANDS
turns.text: That's pre- that's precisely correct,

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Your Honor. We have in this particular case...

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: trouble with the proposition you just stated, but this case is with an with an unadjudicated grievance or or grievance with, that had not been fully determined.

turns.name: HENK BRANDS
turns.text: Precisely.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Mr. Brands,

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: as I understand it, the board, MSPB, applies its so-called Bolling rule.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: to meet...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: the statute?

turns.name: HENK BRANDS
turns.text: We th- we don't think it is enough in a situation like this situation where the prior is being grieved, and here's...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: why.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: challenge the Bolling rule? Is that something we've been asked to review here?

turns.name: HENK BRANDS
turns.text: Well, the Bolling rule is squarely before the Court.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: wrong...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: assert that he- if if the board applies its Bolling standard, that is not enough to s- satisfy the statutory burden of proof?

turns.name: HENK BRANDS
turns.text: We think it is enough in a situation where the prior has become final on its own steam.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: the burden.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: if you view the prior disciplinary action taken by the employer as final, subject only to some kind of collateral attack in a grievance procedure, then it is final unless, at the end of the day, the grievance procedure is successful.

turns.name: HENK BRANDS
turns.text: That is not how we would characterize it.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: I think not collateral estoppel.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Well, we...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: otherwise final action.

turns.name: HENK BRANDS
turns.text: We would not view it as a collateral attack.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Justice Ginsburg asked...

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: Government.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: for my suggestion...

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: that you would hold the second...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: proceeding...

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: in abeyance would apply.

turns.name: HENK BRANDS
turns.text: Very well.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: I'm I'm guilty.

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: when you answer the question, would would you tell us why it would make any difference whether it is whether there's the collateral proceeding going on, whether there's a direct attack going on? Why isn't there a presumption of regularity that attends the Government's action until it is, in fact, overturned as a result of the arbitration...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: process?

turns.name: HENK BRANDS
turns.text: We think there's no presumption of regularity because when the action comes for- before the board or before an arbitrator itself, it is not entitled to any reg-

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: presumption of regularity.

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: the arbitration proceeding.

turns.name: HENK BRANDS
turns.text: Simply for this reaner- reason, Justice Justice Souter.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: the evidence.

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: that's the but I mean, that gets us back to the question we keep going back and forth on.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: or do they have to...

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: prove the existence of the infraction in substance just as if they were proving it as part of the current charge? And it seems odd to me to say that they would have to prove it just as much as if it were part of the current charge.

turns.name: HENK BRANDS
turns.text: Well, I would I would distinguish between three situations.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Well, I'm not saying that...

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: collateral estoppel applies.

turns.name: HENK BRANDS
turns.text: That is correct, although that burden will be very easy to discharge in a case where a prior has already become final or, for that matter, where a prinor- prior was never attacked.

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: There is one exception...

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: be no presumption of regularity.

turns.name: HENK BRANDS
turns.text: Presumption of regularity are applied to a Government action that itself is entitled to deference when it is reviewed by a court.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: talking about...

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: question.

turns.name: HENK BRANDS
turns.text: The Government action, if it is taken to the MSPB, is not entitled to any deference at all because the Government bears the burden of proving de novo that what happened actually occurred.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: not entitled to...

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: you're saying is that the that the aggravating fact is subject to exactly the same burden of proof that the specific instance of of later conduct is is is subject to, the the the instance that gets us before the board on appeal anyway.

turns.name: HENK BRANDS
turns.text: That's precisely correct, although as I said, in two situations that is a burden of proof that should...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: be very easy to discharge. It's on in that...

turns.name: HENK BRANDS
turns.text: third situation.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: Well, but if if you're talking about judicial proceedings, I don't think those rules would necessarily carry over to this sort of rather low level administrative proceeding.

turns.name: HENK BRANDS
turns.text: Your Honor, far be it for me, of course, to to argue that collateral estoppel should apply, but the MSPB, for example, has applied collateral estoppel to prior final arbitral orders, and we don't necessarily see anything wrong with that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: actually occurred.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: to say that the prior is rev- reviewed de novo, I'm I'm not sure that that is an entirely accurate statement because it was a prior that was left unchallenged, I take it, at the time.

turns.name: HENK BRANDS
turns.text: No, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: as...

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: your statement then is limited towards the sort of prior which is un- under review.

turns.name: HENK BRANDS
turns.text: That's that's precisely correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: What is your...

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: what if the employer says there is a prior here, as well as the current conduct, that has never been grieved, n- never been challenged?

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Well, in that...

turns.name: HENK BRANDS
turns.text: situation, it would ordinarily be reasonable for the Government to argue and the MSPB to to agree that it's reasonable to assume that a preponderance of the evidence exists in such a situation because if the if the employee thought that a preponderance did not exist, then he or she would probably have taken it to the MSPB or to an...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: arbitrator. The...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: board has to assume that.

turns.name: HENK BRANDS
turns.text: Well, the agency is not is not s- the question of whether or not the agency may simply level a charge is not subject to this burden of proof.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: before the MSPB.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: any other situation in which the the r- the ref- the re- the review of agency action is conducted on a basis more demanding than the agency action itself?

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Your Honor...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: the agency ignored the fact that that that a grievance was pending, you assert that would have been entirely lawful.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Well, that then...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: when it's on appeal to the Merit Systems Protections Board, you say the Merit Systems Protection Board can reverse the agency because at that stage, suddenly the fact of the prior disciplinary action is not determinative.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: I Th- That's it's very strange. I don't know...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: any other instance in administrative law where what the agency does is right, but on appeal it's wrong.

turns.name: HENK BRANDS
turns.text: I would not characterize this as an appeal.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: However, when the...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: go on.

turns.name: HENK BRANDS
turns.text: When the agency levels its charges, brings its charges in the first place, it does not have to worry, do we ha- can we sustain the burden of proof.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Well, of course, it has to worry about the burden of proof.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: I don't think so, Your Honor.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: this evidence is g- is is good evidence before the agency, the mere fact of the prior disciplinary action, but it suddenly becomes bad evidence before the Merit System Protection Board.

turns.name: HENK BRANDS
turns.text: Well, it's not bad evidence.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: preponderance when it comes in the MS PB...

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: Throughout the admis- the proceedings for the Merit System Protection Board, in the d- initial decision of the administra- it u- the petitioner's referred to the respondent's referred to as the appellant.

turns.name: HENK BRANDS
turns.text: Well, Your Honor, it i- that is true.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: Where did the Federal circuit get that from?

turns.name: HENK BRANDS
turns.text: That is simply because before the Federal cir- before the MSPB, the burden is on the Government agency to prove its charges and review is de novo.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: is on the Government.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: where is section seventy-seven oh one B in the material before us in the briefs?

turns.name: HENK BRANDS
turns.text: It's in the appendix to the petition, page fifty-one, and we refer to it in our our claim rested on the on this on this burden of proof in...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: a woman who works for an agency is late for the twentieth time.

turns.name: HENK BRANDS
turns.text: The Government can fire her.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: All right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: of course, then...

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: the as I read the Federal circuit, it says just what you say.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: The Gov- the Government...

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: cannot use them.

turns.name: HENK BRANDS
turns.text: No, I'm not saying that.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: Right,

turns.name: HENK BRANDS
turns.text: and because you did it nineteen times before, we're firing you today.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: HENK BRANDS
turns.text: Now, however, when that removal comes to the MSPB,

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: Mhm...

turns.name: HENK BRANDS
turns.text: in the ordinary case, those nineteen priors will have become final.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: All right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: What we think is...

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: So so, that's their approach.

turns.name: HENK BRANDS
turns.text: It's not a question of reasonableness, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: says.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: that that statute seems to be talking about cross referencing B.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: That is -- Your Honor...

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: is this piece of paper, which says you were convicted nineteen times before there're nineteen pieces of paper, okay whether that counts as evidence towards the preponderance to support what they did now.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: And...

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: you could make the argument either way.

turns.name: HENK BRANDS
turns.text: Respectfully, Your Honor, that is not how the MSPB itself has interpreted section seventy-seven oh one C one B.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: any aggravating...

turns.name: HENK BRANDS
turns.text: circumstance, and in our view, it couldn't really have been, be any other way.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: Well, now, Mr. Brands, we're reviewing the Federal circuit's decision here, not the MSPB's decision.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: expressing now, into its opinion?

turns.name: HENK BRANDS
turns.text: Yes, it did, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Whereabouts?

turns.name: HENK BRANDS
turns.text: what I was referring to when I was referring to that phrase on page seven of the appendix to the petition.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Well, h- how can you tell...

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: How can you tell th- that one cryptic phrase how can you tell that's what it meant by that?

turns.name: HENK BRANDS
turns.text: Well, it actually said it twice, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: even though...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: proven.

turns.name: HENK BRANDS
turns.text: That's actually not correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: I- I- I- I- Is that right? I mean I d-

turns.name: HENK BRANDS
turns.text: Oh, absolutely.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: These priors have never gone...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: can say, well, it wasn't proven, but you know, I think we ought to put this on your record anyway.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Well, the agency adjudicator...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: has to find, by a preponderance of the evidence, that the employee was guilty of the alleged infraction.

turns.name: HENK BRANDS
turns.text: But the question is whether whether there's any reason for the MSPB to defer to that, and we submit no.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: That's fine...

turns.name: HENK BRANDS
turns.text: prosecutor coming before a trial court.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: more deference...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: we're talking -- What we're talking about here is is sentencing...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: then you should say that even when there is no grievance, the board should not take account of the prior of the prior disciplinary...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: If I...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: you really believe that...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: you would say even if it's not being grieved.

turns.name: HENK BRANDS
turns.text: Justice Scalia, I'm I I wouldn't would, p- I wouldn't put it right th- that way or precisely that way.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: If in that situation...

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: be- m- before you finish, I'd like you to answer the Government's assertion that your neat solution, which is, agency, you can fire this persons, but MSPB must abide the grievance.

turns.name: HENK BRANDS
turns.text: Well, i- i- it's an argument that's raised for the first time today here at the lectern.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: The burden, of course, is...

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: it does weaken your argument that the Government serves its purpose.

turns.name: HENK BRANDS
turns.text: Well, I don't think it quite weakens our argument, Your Honor, because we're talking about letter carriers in this particular case, for example.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: why the government...

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: briefs correct in telling us there are a hundred and twenty-six thousand pending grievances in the postal system?

turns.name: HENK BRANDS
turns.text: The short answer is no, if the allegation is that those are disciplinary grievances.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Brands.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: Mr. Garre, you have two minutes remaining.

turns.name: GREGORY G. GARRE
turns.text: The Federal circuit ruling in this case is not based in any way on the burden of proof applied in board proceedings.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Garre.
